other_observers: Joey Kellner, Steve Stachowiak, Glenn Walbek, Ira Sanders, Charlie Lawrence, Dick Schottler, Randy Lentz, and many others.; Steve Stachowiak originally found the bird, and it was first identified by him, Joey Kellner, and myself.
description: The bird was an obvious gull, a little smaller than the size of the Ring-billed Gulls it was standing next to. The overall shape was fairly slender and attenuated, with long wings sticking out well beyond the tail, and a smaller head than RBGU giving it a more slender look than RBGU.   The face had a patch of black roughly rectangular shaped, and going from the eye to the nape, somewhat reminiscent of the face-mark of a phalarope. The crown, throat, and the rest of the face were white. The nape and back were a medium-dark slate-gray, and this gray color extended out onto the sides of the chest and upper flanks. The wing coverts were a mixture of medium brown and the gray color of the back, with the gray color predominating. The belly and lower flanks were white. The wing-tips were completely black, and with no white between the black and the brown/gray of the wing coverts. In flight, the black on the wing looked to be a between a third and half the wing. The tail was white with a very large, bold black tail band, that was at least the terminal third of the tail. In flight some very faint dusky markings could be seen on the underwings, but not as many as I would expect for this plumage. This may be due to the fact that I never got a great look at the underwing.  The bill was fairly long (a little more than half the head length), and black. It was heavier than I would expect for Franklin's Gull, and had a very slight droop towards the tip. The legs were entirely black.Behaviors: The bird spent most of its time loafing around on sandbars with other gulls, though it sometimes flew over the reservoir, and would dissapear for long periods to who knows where.Call: none heardPlumage: 1st basic
